key_insight: In arsenene, interlayer bonding is not set by registry alone: layer thickness and coordination drive a crossover from larger-spacing few-layer structures to compact bulk-like stacking, with a matching semiconductor-to-semimetal transition.
engineering_relevance: For 2D device and heterostructure design, the paper shows that thickness-dependent stacking can strongly alter spacing, coupling, and band gap, so bulk assumptions may fail at the few-layer limit.
method: Combined diffusion quantum Monte Carlo (DMC) and density functional theory (DFT) to benchmark interlayer separations and stacking energetics.
Compared multiple exchange-correlation functionals and found SCAN+rVV10 best matched DMC.
Used DMC-benchmarked SCAN+rVV10 to predict a thickness-driven stacking sequence and electronic crossover.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that DMC establishes bulk gray arsenic as compact while few-layer structures stay at larger separations, and that SCAN+rVV10 most closely reproduces DMC equilibrium separations and stacking energetics.
watch_item: The predicted stacking and band-gap crossover depend on functional-based electronic structure modeling, so experimental confirmation of the thickness-dependent sequence and semimetal onset is important.
paper_title: Thickness-Dependent Interlayer Coupling and Semiconductor-to-Semimetal Crossover in Arsenene Multilayers
